annotations.label: Algorithm

anchor.snippet: of mathematically rigorous instructions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no informal/general definition of "algorithm"; it formalizes specific computation models, not the umbrella concept.

annotations.label: Heuristic

anchor.snippet: a heuristic is an approach to solving problems

annotations.note: The notion of a heuristic is not a formalized object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effective method

anchor.snippet: an algorithm can be expressed within a finite amount of space and time

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes computability (partial recursive functions) but not the informal Church-Turing notion of "effective method".

annotations.label: Informal definition of algorithm

anchor.snippet: a set of rules that precisely defines a sequence of operations

annotations.note: An informal prose definition has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Program is an algorithm only if it halts

anchor.snippet: a program is an algorithm only if it stops eventually

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the halting predicate and the undecidability of the halting problem, but not the definitional claim that an algorithm must halt.

annotations.label: Formal definition of algorithm

anchor.snippet: algorithm is an explicit set of instructions to produce an output

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes concrete models of computation (Turing machines, partial recursive functions) that instantiate this idea, but not a single abstract "formal definition of algorithm".

annotations.label: High-level description

anchor.snippet: A high-level description describes the qualities of the algorithm itself

annotations.note: This is a meta-level description style with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Implementation description

anchor.snippet: An implementation description describes the general manner in which the machine moves its head

annotations.note: A description-level concept, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal description

anchor.snippet: a formal description gives the exact state table and list of transitions

annotations.note: Mathlib's Turing machine is defined exactly by states, configurations, and a transition step function, matching the idea of a state table with transitions.

annotations.label: Flowchart

anchor.snippet: A flowchart is a graphical aid that describes and documents an algorithm

annotations.note: Flowcharts are a diagrammatic notation with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Linear-time list summation

anchor.snippet: an algorithm that adds up the elements of a list of n numbers

annotations.note: The summation function `List.sum` exists, but its linear-time complexity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Binary search vs sequential search

anchor.snippet: outperforms a sequential search

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalized search algorithms with comparative running-time analysis.

annotations.label: Best case

anchor.snippet: The best case of an algorithm refers to the scenario or input

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complexity-analysis framework defining best-case behavior.

annotations.label: Worst case

anchor.snippet: The worst case of an algorithm is the case that causes the algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complexity-analysis framework defining worst-case behavior.

annotations.label: Turing-complete models compute any algorithm

anchor.snippet: Any algorithm can be computed by any Turing complete model

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a Turing machine can evaluate any partial recursive program (and the reverse), giving model equivalence, but no general statement about "any Turing-complete model".

annotations.label: Four instruction types for Turing completeness

anchor.snippet: Turing completeness only requires four instruction types

annotations.note: This specific claim about a four-instruction basis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Böhm-Jacopini canonical structures

anchor.snippet: Tausworthe augments the three Böhm-Jacopini canonical structures

annotations.note: The Böhm-Jacopini structured-programming structures are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Structured programs admit proofs by induction

anchor.snippet: it lends itself to proofs of correctness using mathematical induction

annotations.note: While Mathlib uses induction pervasively, this meta-statement about structured-program correctness proofs is not itself formalized.

annotations.label: Recursive algorithm

anchor.snippet: A recursive algorithm invokes itself repeatedly until meeting a termination condition

annotations.note: Recursion is a language/meta feature; "recursive algorithm" is not a formalized mathematical object.

annotations.label: Iterative algorithm

anchor.snippet: Iterative algorithms use repetitions such as loops or data structures like stacks

annotations.note: The concept of an iterative algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tower of Hanoi

anchor.snippet: The Tower of Hanoi is a puzzle commonly solved using recursive implementation

annotations.note: The Tower of Hanoi puzzle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive and iterative versions are equivalent

anchor.snippet: Every recursive version has an equivalent

annotations.note: No general recursion/iteration equivalence theorem is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Serial algorithm

anchor.snippet: Serial algorithms are designed for these environments

annotations.note: Serial-execution algorithms are not a formalized concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallel algorithm

anchor.snippet: Parallel algorithms take advantage of computer architectures

annotations.note: Parallel algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distributed algorithm

anchor.snippet: Distributed algorithms use multiple machines connected via a computer network

annotations.note: Distributed algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inherently serial problems

anchor.snippet: are called inherently serial problems

annotations.note: This complexity-theory notion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deterministic vs non-deterministic algorithms

anchor.snippet: Deterministic algorithms solve the problem with exact decisions at every step

annotations.note: Mathlib has deterministic/non-deterministic finite automata but no general deterministic-vs-nondeterministic algorithm definition.

annotations.label: Approximation algorithm

anchor.snippet: approximation algorithms seek an approximation that is close to the true solution

annotations.note: Approximation algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Knapsack problem

anchor.snippet: the goal is to pack the knapsack to get the maximum total value

annotations.note: The knapsack problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum algorithm

anchor.snippet: Quantum algorithms run on a realistic model of quantum computation

annotations.note: Quantum computation models and quantum algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brute force

anchor.snippet: Brute force is a problem-solving method of systematically trying every possible option

annotations.note: The brute-force design paradigm is not a formalized object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Divide-and-conquer

anchor.snippet: A divide-and-conquer algorithm repeatedly reduces a problem to one or more smaller instances of itself

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Akra-Bazzi theorem for the asymptotic cost of divide-and-conquer recurrences, but not the paradigm definition itself.

annotations.label: Merge sort

anchor.snippet: Merge sorting is an example of divide and conquer

annotations.note: `List.mergeSort` is the merge sort algorithm, with proofs of sortedness and permutation invariance.

annotations.label: Prune and search / decrease-and-conquer

anchor.snippet: called prune and search or decrease-and-conquer algorithm

annotations.note: The prune-and-search/decrease-and-conquer paradigm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary search as prune and search

anchor.snippet: An example of a prune and search algorithm is the binary search algorithm

annotations.note: Binary search is not formalized as an algorithm in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph exploration algorithm

anchor.snippet: A graph exploration algorithm specifies rules for moving around a graph

annotations.note: Mathlib has graph theory but no formalized graph exploration/traversal algorithm concept.

annotations.label: Randomized algorithm

anchor.snippet: Such algorithms make some choices randomly

annotations.note: Randomized algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P versus NP problem

anchor.snippet: is an open question known as the P versus NP problem

annotations.note: The P versus NP problem and its complexity classes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monte Carlo algorithm

anchor.snippet: Monte Carlo algorithms return a correct answer with high probability

annotations.note: Monte Carlo algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Las Vegas algorithm

anchor.snippet: Las Vegas algorithms always return the correct answer

annotations.note: Las Vegas algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction of complexity (transform and conquer)

anchor.snippet: This technique transforms difficult problems into better-known problems

annotations.note: Mathlib has computability many-one reductions but no formalized algorithmic transform-and-conquer technique.

annotations.label: Median via sorting selection algorithm

anchor.snippet: one selection algorithm finds the median of an unsorted list

annotations.note: No median/selection algorithm is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Backtracking

anchor.snippet: multiple solutions are built incrementally and abandoned

annotations.note: The backtracking paradigm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear programming

anchor.snippet: searching for optimal solutions to a linear function bound by linear equality and inequality constraints

annotations.note: Mathlib has convex analysis but no formalized linear programming problem/solver.

annotations.label: Integer programming

anchor.snippet: then it is classified in integer programming

annotations.note: Integer programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dynamic programming

anchor.snippet: a quicker approach called dynamic programming avoids recomputing solutions

annotations.note: The dynamic programming paradigm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Floyd–Warshall shortest path

anchor.snippet: the shortest path between a start and goal vertex in a weighted graph can be found

annotations.note: The Floyd–Warshall shortest-path algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Greedy algorithm

anchor.snippet: work by examining substructures, in this case not of the problem but of a given solution

annotations.note: The greedy-algorithm paradigm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heuristic algorithm (optimization)

anchor.snippet: heuristic algorithms find solutions close to the optimal solution

annotations.note: Heuristic optimization algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finding the largest number in a list

anchor.snippet: One of the simplest algorithms finds the largest number in a list of numbers

annotations.note: `List.maximum` (and `List.argmax`) compute the largest element of a list, but the result is a function/lemma rather than an algorithm with the described iterative procedure.
